result:
I keep thinking about the way light looks when it’s almost gone—how it clings to the edges of things like it’s afraid to leave. A rim of gold on a rooftop, a single silver stitch on the hem of a cloud. There’s a hush that arrives with it, not quite silence, more like the world holding its breath before a secret is spoken. I have never been able to decide whether that moment feels like mercy or warning.

Yesterday I watched a neighbor coax a reluctant tomato vine back into its cage. She talked to it the whole time, half lullaby, half complaint, the way my grandmother used to scold the biscuits while she kneaded them. I stood behind the slats of my blinds like some amateur spy, caught between trespass and tenderness. It struck me that almost everything I love begins this way: covert observation, unreasonable loyalty to small, perishable details. A woman’s wrist turning under the faucet. The hush again. The way the water caught the last of the light and threw it back at the sky, as if returning a borrowed coat.

I wonder what becomes of all that spilled luminosity when the bulbs burn out and the sun clocks out for good. Does it pool somewhere, an unseen reservoir of afterglow? Do moths sip from it when we’re not looking? I imagine a quiet bar of phosphorescence where fireflies go to brag about their childhoods. They trade stories of mason jars with nail holes punched in the lid, of the first human palm that ever closed around them like a sudden moon. Last call is dawn; they stagger home drunk on memory, thoraxes buzzing off-key.

Inside my own apartment the air tastes of coffee grounds and the ghost of a cinnamon candle. I keep rearranging the same five books on the windowsill, as though the right sequence might open a portal. So far the only magic is that the dust motes seem to dance more willingly when Joni Mitchell sings. I rotate a paperweight galaxy and pretend I’m choreographing new constellations: The Teacup, The Unanswered Email, The Emergency Contact Who Never Picks Up. They glitter spitefully, refusing to grant wishes.

There’s a crack in the plaster shaped vaguely like the Iberian Peninsula. I trace it when I can’t sleep, narrate tiny histories to its coastline. Invisible explorers beach their ships at my fingertip, plant flags of insomnia, name the conquered territory 3:17 A.M. They import citrus and dread, export excuses and half-finished poems. Their maps are tattooed on the inside of my eyelids; I sail them until the alarm goes off.

Sometimes I think the body is just a rental unit for nostalgia. Landlord: Time. Pet policy: one ephemeral longing per tenant. Noise ordinance: heart must be kept below sixty decibels after ten. I pay my dues in bruises I don’t remember collecting, in the soft collapse of cartilage every time I kneel to pick up another piece of lint that looks, from the corner of an exhausted eye, like your face.

I read once that scientists taught rhesus monkeys to use money, and the first thing they did was pay for sex. The second thing they did was throw it around like confetti when no one was watching. I keep that fact folded in my wallet like emergency currency. On bad days I take it out, smooth the creases, remind myself that even primates understand the ceremonial futility of tender. We all want to be rich in something that can’t be kept.

Tonight the moon is a clipped fingernail, low and careless. I stand on the fire escape trying to bet on which window across the alley will blink off last. It’s a stupid game; everybody wins eventually. Somewhere below, a delivery bike chain chatters like cheap jewelry. The rider’s reflective vest catches my porch light and for a second he is incandescent, a comet with dumplings. I salute him, knight of the hungry and thelonely. He lifts a hand without looking back, already trading his glow for the next address.

I go back in, leave the door unlatched. Maybe the crack in the wall will widen enough for Portugal to slip through, carrying its saudade on a raft of salt. Maybe the tomatoes will crawl all the way here, listening for their names. Maybe the moon will grow back, and the light will decide to stay, even if it has to shrink itself to the size of a dust mote, even if it has to live on the tip of a tongue that never quite finishes saying I miss you.

Until then I keep the stove light on, a small yellow debt I owe to the idea of return. It hums like a refrigerator monk, reciting the same three words until they stop meaning anything and start meaning everything: come home, come home, come home.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
